problem_statement: Vibe coding pushes developers to accept AI-generated code with little to no code reading, delegating high-level design decisions to AI; the paper examines how developers oversee, verify, and regulate trust in this AI-generated code and where that oversight breaks down.
magnitude_evidence: r/vibecoding, created six days after the term was coined, 'has already grown to 156,000 members, ranking in the top 1% of Reddit communities'; analysis of over 5,000 comments across 134 posts plus 11 interviews
insufficiency_evidence: 'Teams need processes for this and cannot outsource verification to the same models that generate code'; delegating review to AI self-audit 'signals high trust in model ability; it is unclear how effective these strategies are compared to traditional code review'; 'My 400-line code is now 3000 lines and neither of us can read it anymore' (R63)
solution_mechanisms: trust calibration by project risk level
reserve vibe coding for low-stakes/personal projects
key_constructs: four-component theory of vibe coding (interaction, co-creation, flow, trust)
notes: Qualitative study of social-media posts and interviews; oversight/governance is descriptive from practitioner discourse, not formal policy; no named regulatory frameworks despite mentions of data-protection laws and regulators.
